title: Troubling new details emerge on diabetes ouster controversy
gist: New preprint articles from Diabetes Care's deputy editors allege the American Diabetes Association staged a premeditated, quota-driven ambush to remove five scientists distributing a research-funding-cut editorial at its June 2026 conference, and that a month later the ADA has taken no corrective action despite an earlier CEO apology.

themes.theme: The New Orleans ouster and ADA's shifting justifications

insights.claim: On June 5, 2026, ADA security and police removed five scientists (Steven Kahn, Desmond Schatz, Aaron Kelly, Justin Ryder, Irl Hirsch) plus a sixth, Maureen Gannon, from the American Diabetes Association's New Orleans conference after they distributed copies of a Diabetes Care editorial critical of the Trump administration's cuts to scientific research; police confiscated their badges and threatened arrest if they returned.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Shows how a nonprofit medical society used security/police force against its own members and editors over a policy-critical publication, a governance failure with reputational and funding-advocacy implications for the organization.
insights.quote: Within minutes of handing out the editorial, police reportedly escorted the scientists out of the conference in New Orleans.

insights.claim: ADA gave at least three different, shifting justifications for the ouster in succession: first that the scientists violated the conference code of conduct, then that it was 'not because of the viewpoints expressed,' and finally that it was required by federal 501(c)(3) nonpartisanship rules.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A shifting official rationale is a classic tell of institutional overreach being retrofitted with justification; useful pattern-match for evaluating other institutional-accountability controversies.
insights.locator: At first, the ADA tried to justify its decision

insights.claim: The federal 501(c)(3) nonpartisanship regulations that ADA cited to justify the removals do not actually restrict organization leaders from sharing political views in a personal capacity or speaking on public policy issues, per the article's account.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: The legal justification offered publicly appears misapplied, undermining ADA's defense and raising the risk of further reputational or governance fallout if challenged.
insights.quote: However, the federal regulations do not restrict leaders of organizations from sharing political views in a personal capacity or from speaking on important public policy issues.

insights.claim: A month after CEO Charles Henderson's video apology and promise of 'a thorough independent review,' the New Orleans Five report no leadership meeting, no formal apology beyond the video, no exoneration, no corrective actions, no resignations of staff involved, and no identification of who ordered the removals.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Promised accountability measures have stalled a month out, a signal that the controversy is likely to keep escalating rather than resolve quietly.

insights.claim: The New Orleans Five are demanding the disclaimers attached to the published Diabetes Care editorials be removed, alongside a formal investigation and a process for professional membership concerns.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Indicates the dispute is now also about editorial independence and control over the journal's content, not just the conference incident itself.
insights.quote: Any disclaimers need to be removed from the published editorials.
insights.locator: The five are requesting the promised thorough investigation

insights.claim: Deputy editors' preprint alleges ADA leadership knew in advance that scientists would distribute the editorial: Desmond Schatz texted Chief Scientific and Medical Officer Rita Kalyani beforehand asking her to warn Steven Kahn where he could and couldn't hand out copies, but Kalyani said she was on a plane and never followed up.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Prior knowledge without intervention undercuts ADA's claim that the removal was a routine, unplanned enforcement action.

insights.claim: As four scientists were being removed, a police officer asked the head of conference security whether 'that was sufficient'; the security officer said he needed 'one more badge' and directed them to remove a fifth person, Maureen Gannon, suggesting a predetermined quota coordinated with ADA, referred to by security as 'the client.'
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A quota-style removal process is strong circumstantial evidence of premeditation rather than an ad hoc code-of-conduct response, strengthening the case for external investigation.

insights.claim: John Buse, who chaired the symposium in Kahn's place, alleges the ADA's actions were 'a premeditated attack on Steven Kahn and other attendees' constituting 'an existential threat to the ADA,' comparing the tactics to federal government efforts to suppress protest.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A senior, previously ADA-aligned faculty member's willingness to frame the incident as an existential governance crisis signals deep internal fracture likely to affect membership retention and fundraising.
insights.quote: It was an ambush. ADA leadership knew exactly what was going to happen and when.

themes.theme: Institutional root cause: the 2025 panel dispute

insights.claim: Deputy editors Elizabeth Selvin and Cheryl A.M. Anderson trace the current conflict back to a 2025 ADA session, 'How Do We Fix a Broken Health Care System?', featuring Rep. Kim Schrier, which ADA leadership deemed 'unbalanced' and asked to be rebalanced with opposing-view speakers, then approved but declined to advertise.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: The June 5 conference incident wasn't an isolated overreaction but the culmination of a year-long tension between ADA leadership and its journal editors over political content, useful context for judging how the dispute will resolve.
insights.locator: Per deputy editors Elizabeth Selvin and Cheryl A.M. Anderson

insights.claim: Following the June 5 incident, ADA leadership canceled the annual in-person meetings between ADA and the Diabetes Care editorial board and associate editors, removing what the deputy editors call 'the only opportunity we have all year to connect face-to-face with the full editorial team.'
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Cutting off the editorial board's main communication channel with the parent organization escalates rather than de-escalates the conflict, working against ADA's stated healing intentions.
insights.quote: the only opportunity we have all year to connect face-to-face with the full editorial team
insights.locator: Per deputy editors Elizabeth Selvin and Cheryl A.M. Anderson

insights.claim: Selvin and Anderson note the irony that ADA has since hired a public relations crisis firm and is now emailing members advocacy messages opposing harmful federal policy changes — the same positions the journal editorial was punished for expressing.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: The organization's own subsequent advocacy conduct contradicts its stated rationale for the ouster, a vulnerability that could be used in the demanded independent review or in legal/reputational challenges.
insights.quote: Now, with a public relations crisis firm in tow, the ADA has emailed advocacy messages opposing federal policies and is taking strong stances on proposed changes that would be harmful to diabetes patients and research.
insights.locator: Per deputy editors Elizabeth Selvin and Cheryl A.M. Anderson

insights.claim: Mark Atkinson resigned as chair of the ADA's Scientific Sessions Meeting Planning Committee after a letter defending the ADA's actions was released bearing his name and the CEO's, despite Atkinson never having agreed to the final text he says was substantially different from what he reviewed.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Internal process breakdowns (unauthorized use of a committee chair's name) point to leadership dysfunction beyond the conference incident itself.
insights.quote: a substantially different letter was released, bearing my name and that of the ADA's chief executive officer
insights.locator: Mark Atkinson, chair of the Scientific Sessions Meeting Planning Committee

insights.claim: More than 40 ADA officials signed a letter calling the ouster decision 'outrageous' and ADA's justifications 'unpersuasive' and 'fatuous nonsense,' demanding an immediate and unconditional public apology and a review of the incident.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Signals broad, senior-level internal revolt within the organization, not just external criticism — a governance risk indicator for anyone tracking ADA's institutional stability.
insights.locator: Backlash to the ADA's actions spread rapidly

insights.claim: An open letter to ADA titled 'Shame on You' had gathered over 7,500 signatures by publication time, and social-media backlash sharply increased page views of the original April editorial, illustrating a Streisand-effect dynamic where suppression amplified the message's reach.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: A generalizable pattern: institutional attempts to suppress scientific or political speech tend to backfire virally, amplifying the very message the institution sought to contain.
insights.locator: Backlash to the ADA's actions spread rapidly
